audit_trail.notes: All three reviewers participated in both cycles. Scores were stable across cycles (mechanical averages: cycle 1 = 5.567, cycle 2 = 5.567). The principal cross-cycle update was claude explicitly invalidating the ℏ² typographic concern raised by codex as an HTML artifact, and gemini adding a direct rebuttal to claude's dwell-time argument. No reviewer changed their verdict between cycles. The largest disagreement is between gemini (fatal flaw in Bohmian challenge) and claude (dwell-time argument independently valid); codex occupies a middle position. gemini's fatal objection is logically well-grounded (quantum equilibrium hypothesis) and was not substantively rebutted in either review cycle.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper reports a sophisticated optical experiment that measures how fast quantum particles move through regions they are classically forbidden to enter, finding — counterintuitively — that particles with less energy move faster inside the barrier. The authors argue that this result challenges Bohmian mechanics, an alternative interpretation of quantum physics where particles follow definite hidden trajectories. Reviewers agree the core speed measurements are technically clean and reproducible, but disagree sharply on whether the experiment actually tests Bohmian mechanics, since Bohmian mechanics is mathematically constructed to reproduce all measurable quantum statistics and does not claim the coupled-waveguide clock readout equals its trajectory velocity. The most important caveat for a general reader is that demonstrating a conflict between an operationally defined speed and a theoretical velocity concept may not constitute an empirical refutation of the underlying interpretation.
human_spotchecks: Verify whether the coupled Schrödinger equations (2) and (3) in the official published PDF contain ℏ² (not ℏ) in the kinetic term, as the HTML source appears to have lost a superscript.

Examine reference 35 (Klaers, Sharoglazova & Toebes, Phys. Rev. A 107, 052201 (2023)) to determine whether it establishes the validity of k₁ = mJ₀/(ℏk₂) in the evanescent regime with quantitative error bounds, and whether it quantifies how coupling J₀ perturbs evanescent states near the threshold |Δ| ~ ℏJ₀.

Check whether the source data (figshare DOI 10.6084/m9.figshare.28847645) support fitting the evanescent-regime population curves with the full functional form ρ_a = sinh²(J₀x/v)/[sinh² + cosh²] as an alternative to the parabolic approximation, to independently validate the 6.7% systematic correction estimate.

Assess the peer review supplementary PDF (linked in the paper) to determine whether referees raised the objection that the measurement apparatus (auxiliary waveguide + coupling) is itself a quantum system that modifies Bohmian trajectories, and how the authors responded.

Determine which Bohmian formalism for photons or electromagnetic fields (e.g., Bialynicki-Birula field ontology, effective-mass guiding equation, or other) is the correct Bohmian prediction for paraxial photons in a planar optical microcavity, and whether that formalism also predicts v_S = 0 in the evanescent regime.

Assess whether a full Bohmian treatment of the combined system — including the auxiliary waveguide, continuous quasi-stationary lasing preparation, finite photon lifetime (τ ≈ 270 ps), and imaging detection — changes the Bohmian prediction in a way that would resolve or sharpen the claimed conflict.
public_summary: Sharoglazova et al. (Nature 643, 2025) report measurements of particle motion in classically forbidden quantum states using a photon Bose–Einstein condensation microcavity with a nanostructured double-waveguide geometry. The core experimental finding — that the decay constant of evanescent wave functions corresponds to a mirror-symmetric speed scale v = √(2|Δ|/m) — is reproducible over 156 independent measurements with carefully quantified systematic errors. The measurement scheme converts population transfer between coupled waveguides into a spatio-temporal speed proxy, and the authors further validate the result via a semiclassical dwell-time estimate. An interferometric measurement independently confirms that the phase-gradient (Bohmian) velocity v_S is consistent with zero in the evanescent regime, matching the standard Bohmian prediction for a purely real-valued eigenfunction. The paper argues this constitutes a challenge to Bohmian mechanics, but reviewers flag two significant interpretive gaps: (1) the comparison applies Bohmian mechanics to the isolated step state rather than the full coupled measurement apparatus, and (2) Bohmian mechanics reproduces all Born-rule position statistics by construction, so measurements of photon density distributions alone are insufficient to discriminate it from standard quantum mechanics. The experiment is technically impressive and the tunnelling-time implications (evanescent de Broglie relation, Büttiker–Landauer time) are well-motivated; the strength of the anti-Bohmian conclusion is contested.
reviewer_disagreements: Dwell-time argument strength: claude rates it 'well_supported' and the paper's 'strongest contribution', arguing it is independent of the speed interpretation and represents a direct logical inconsistency within Bohmian mechanics; gemini rates it 'major_flaw' and 'unsupported', arguing the quantum equilibrium hypothesis forces Bohmian mechanics to reproduce the same spatial density and thus the same lossless profile, so the contradiction is definitional rather than empirical; codex is intermediate.

Overall verdict: claude recommends 'promising' (6.5/10) on the grounds that the experimental results are clean and the dwell-time argument has independent merit; codex recommends 'contested' (6.2/10) because the speed interpretation and Bohmian comparison are both under-established; gemini recommends 'major_flaw' (4.0/10) because the titular claim relies on a fundamental category error about hidden-variable theories.

Severity of the speed-interpretation objection: codex rates it 'major / would change verdict if true', arguing the operational quantity cannot be equated with particle speed for states with zero net current; claude rates it 'moderate / would not change verdict', arguing the paper is transparent about the operational definition and the mapping is physically motivated by the same equations that describe propagating states.

Dimensional inconsistency in equations (2) and (3) (ℏ vs. ℏ²): claude is confident this is an HTML extraction artifact given surrounding equations and Nature peer review; codex retains it as a human spotcheck item at moderate confidence; gemini gives 0.99 confidence it is a typographical error in the paper.

strongest_objections.objection: Bohmian mechanics is constructed via the quantum equilibrium hypothesis to reproduce all Born-rule position distributions (intensity patterns), which are the only empirical outputs of this experiment. Demonstrating that an operationally defined coupled-mode speed differs from the Bohmian phase-gradient velocity v_S highlights a known interpretive feature of the theory — that operational clock-based speeds need not equal Bohmian trajectory velocities — but does not constitute an empirical refutation of Bohmian mechanics.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract and Conclusion (titular claim); Main text paragraphs 6–8 (Bohmian conflict); Fig. 4c and dwell-time discussion

strongest_objections.objection: The paper claims the measured speed conflicts with Bohmian mechanics, but the Bohmian comparison is applied only to the guiding-equation velocity of the isolated reflective step eigenfunction. A proper Bohmian test requires modelling the full measurement apparatus — auxiliary waveguide, coupling constant J₀, lasing preparation, finite photon lifetime, and imaging — as part of the combined quantum system. Without this analysis, the experiment may show that the clock readout differs from the isolated-state Bohmian velocity, but not that Bohmian mechanics makes a falsified empirical prediction for the actual setup.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Fig. 4 discussion; paragraphs beginning 'The assumption that particles in the step potential are at rest…'; Conclusion

strongest_objections.objection: The inference from coupled-waveguide population growth to 'particle speed' in fully reflective evanescent states is not fully established as a kinematic claim. For evanescent states with zero net current, the fitted quantity v = ℏκ/m is algebraically the spatial decay rate scaled by ℏ/m — an operational parameter of the coupled-mode wave function, not an independently verified trajectory speed of individual particles. The paper is transparent about the operational definition but the attribution to particle motion requires additional justification.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Methods: 'Measurement scheme for determining particle speeds', equations (6)–(8); Main text clock argument

strongest_objections.objection: The experiment uses photons in a paraxial optical microcavity approximated as a 2D massive particle gas, then applies the non-relativistic Bohmian guiding equation v_S = (ℏ/m)∇S directly to photons via effective mass. Multiple inequivalent Bohmian formalisms for photons or electromagnetic fields exist (e.g., Bialynicki-Birula); the paper does not cite or engage with this literature, so whether v_S = 0 is the correct Bohmian prediction for photons in this geometry is not established.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Equation (1), v_S definition; main text on photon effective mass; Abstract: 'a test of Bohmian trajectories'

strongest_objections.objection: The measurement scheme's key derivation k₁ = mJ₀/(ℏk₂) is valid only when |E−V₀| ≫ ℏJ₀, so that k₂ is independent of the coupling J₀. Near the threshold (|Δ| ~ ℏJ₀), this condition breaks down and the extracted speed becomes apparatus-dependent. The paper acknowledges a minimum speed v_min = √(ℏJ₀/m) near threshold, but does not quantify corrections for all reported energy values.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Methods after equation (7); Fig. 3a discussion of near-threshold behaviour and v_min

strongest_objections.objection: The dwell-time inconsistency argument — that Bohmian mechanics implies infinite dwell time, contradicted by the observed lossless evanescent wave function — is disputed as circular. Because Bohmian mechanics must reproduce the same spatial probability density as standard QM, it also predicts the same 'lossless-looking' intensity profile. The apparent contradiction arises from mixing the standard-QM definition of j_in into the Bohmian framework rather than from a genuine empirical divergence.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Main text paragraph: 'The assumption that particles in the step potential are at rest also implies…'; Fig. 3b dwell-time discussion

strongest_supported_claims: The decay constant κ of evanescent photon states at the reflective step closely follows κ = √(2m|Δ|)/ℏ, in excellent agreement with standard quantum mechanics, as confirmed by Fig. 2c across a wide energy range.

The coupled-waveguide population transfer yields a reproducible operational speed that is mirror-symmetric about Δ = 0, agreeing with v = √(2|Δ|/m) for sufficiently large |Δ|, across 156 independent measurements with systematically bounded fitting errors.

The phase-gradient velocity v_S, measured interferometrically via Mach–Zehnder interference with a mirrored image, is consistent with zero in the classically forbidden regime: ⟨v_S⟩ = (59 ± 42) km/s for Δ ≤ −0.05 meV.

The semiclassical dwell-time estimate τ_λ = λ/v, derived from the measured speed and decay length, agrees with the independently calculated quantum mechanical dwell time τ_dwell = N/j_in over a wide range of energies (Fig. 3b), providing internal consistency for the energy–speed relation.

The decay-length relation λ = ℏ/(mv) connects the evanescent decay length to the measured speed scale in a form structurally analogous to a de Broglie relation, following directly from the coupled-mode analysis.
technical_takeaway: The paper uses inter-waveguide population transfer as a spatio-temporal clock to extract an operational speed v = √(2|Δ|/m) for photon states in the classically forbidden regime of a reflective potential step, yielding a decay-length de Broglie relation λ = ℏ/(mv). The energy–speed curve is mirror-symmetric about Δ = 0 across 156 independent measurements. A secondary interferometric measurement confirms v_S ≈ 0 (phase-gradient velocity) in the evanescent regime, consistent with the Bohmian guiding-equation prediction for a real-valued eigenfunction. The claimed Bohmian challenge rests on equating the coupled-waveguide clock speed with the Bohmian trajectory speed — an identification Bohmian mechanics explicitly rejects — and the comparison is applied only to the isolated step state rather than to the full combined system (auxiliary waveguide, coupling J₀, lasing preparation, photon loss). Reviewers are split on whether the paper's dwell-time argument (Bohmian infinite dwell time vs. observed lossless evanescent wave function) constitutes an independent empirical inconsistency or also fails by the quantum equilibrium hypothesis.
